metadata.title: Why sending the full list beats sending only what changed
metadata.purpose: Level delivery makes the receiver "latest message wins" — a dropped message means "stale for a moment," not "permanently wrong." Edge deltas are efficient but fragile: one lost delta corrupts any consumer without a resync path. Redundant but idempotent beats minimal but brittle.
metadata.clock: narration (Kokoro af_kore) — master clock; conform each beat to measured audio
metadata.still_lanes: c2v (edge timeline vs. level timeline), raster (drop marker on each)
metadata.exclusions: WebSocket transport internals, task scheduling APIs, step sequencing
metadata.visual_object: Two timelines — an edge stream that diverges and never recovers after one drop, vs. a level stream that snaps back on the next emission.
metadata.hook: A background workflow emits "task started / task finished" deltas; miss one and your live-task list drifts from reality forever — no error, no recovery.

beats.narration_text: A consumer accumulates `task_started` and removes on completion; one `task_started` is dropped, so it permanently undercounts. `background_tasks_changed` replaces this by emitting the COMPLETE current set on every change.
beats.scaffold_note: from Key case — cold open on the concrete instance

beats.narration_text: Edge events are smaller and send only what changed; so why replace them with a full-set emission on every change?
A background workflow emits "task started / task finished" deltas; miss one and your live-task list drifts from reality forever — no error, no recovery.

Level delivery makes the receiver "latest message wins" — a dropped message means "stale for a moment," not "permanently wrong." Edge deltas are efficient but fragile: one lost delta corrupts any consumer without a resync path. Redundant but idempotent beats minimal but brittle.
beats.visual: Two timelines — an edge stream that diverges and never recovers after one drop, vs. a level stream that snaps back on the next emission.
beats.scaffold_note: the resolving mechanism; orbits the visual object

beats.narration_text: Tasks A, B, C start; `started(C)` is dropped. Edge consumer stuck at {A,B}; level consumer gets `changed([A,B,C])` and stays correct after any delivered message.

Level delivery makes the receiver "latest message wins" — a dropped message means "stale for a moment," not "permanently wrong." Edge deltas are efficient but fragile: one lost delta corrupts any consumer without a resync path. Redundant but idempotent beats minimal but brittle.
